thesis: The meaningful result is not liftoff alone; it is whether Flight 13 completes the repaired booster return sequence, deploys its payload, and relights a Raptor in space.

sources.notes: Official Flight 13 announcement and 60-second montage. Source video retained unchanged; only minimal muted excerpts are used with an on-screen source label.
Official 4K footage of Booster 20 moving to the Starbase pad. Minimal muted excerpt used to identify the vehicle and current preparation.
Official mission page used for the target date and planned test objectives. Publication timing may change.
Official FAA statement closing the Flight 12 mishap investigation and listing the two most probable causes and four corrective actions.
Official NASA program page confirming work with SpaceX on Starship HLS for Artemis III and Artemis IV. Third-party artwork on the page is not reused.

facts.claim: SpaceX is targeting July 16, 2026 for Starship Flight 13.
facts.sensitivity: State this as a target because launch schedules can change.

facts.claim: The FAA says the two most probable causes for the Flight 12 booster loss were heat effects on propulsion-system components and erroneous engine-alarm settings.
facts.sensitivity: Attribute the findings to the FAA's accepted investigation rather than presenting an independent diagnosis.

facts.claim: SpaceX identified four corrective actions after Flight 12, including hardware and software configuration updates.
facts.sensitivity: Do not imply the corrective actions guarantee Flight 13 success.

facts.claim: Flight 13 plans a complete Super Heavy boostback burn and soft splashdown, deployment of twenty Starlink V3 satellites, and an in-space Raptor relight before Starship's descent.
facts.sensitivity: These are planned objectives, not completed achievements.

facts.claim: NASA is working with SpaceX to develop Starship HLS for Artemis III and Artemis IV.
facts.sensitivity: Do not imply this flight alone certifies the lunar lander.
